base_method_text:
{"arxiv_id":"2402.10198v3","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2402.10198v3","title":"SAMformer: Unlocking the Potential of Transformers in Time Series
  Forecasting with Sharpness-Aware Minimization and Channel-Wise Attention","authors":["Romain Ilbert","Ambroise Odonnat","Vasilii Feofanov","Aladin Virmaux","Giuseppe Paolo","Themis Palpanas","Ievgen Redko"],"published_date":"2024-02-15T18:55:05Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Transformer-based architectures achieved breakthrough performance in natural
language processing and computer vision, yet they remain inferior to simpler
linear baselines in multivariate long-term forecasting. To better understand
this phenomenon, we start by studying a toy linear forecasting problem for
which we show that transformers are incapable of converging to their true
solution despite their high expressive power. We further identify the attention
of transformers as being responsible for this low generalization capacity.
Building upon this insight, we propose a shallow lightweight transformer model
that successfully escapes bad local minima when optimized with sharpness-aware
optimization. We empirically demonstrate that this result extends to all
commonly used real-world multivariate time series datasets. In particular,
SAMformer surpasses current state-of-the-art methods and is on par with the
biggest foundation model MOIRAI while having significantly fewer parameters.
The code is available at https://github.com/romilbert/samformer.","github_url":"https://github.com/romilbert/samformer","main_contributions":"The paper addresses the issue of poor generalization of transformer-based architectures in multivariate long-term time series forecasting. It identifies attention mechanisms as a key factor and proposes SAMformer, a shallow transformer model optimized with sharpness-aware minimization (SAM), achieving state-of-the-art performance with fewer parameters.","methodology":"The methodology involves analyzing the loss landscape of transformers, identifying entropy collapse and sharpness as issues. The proposed SAMformer model incorporates reversible instance normalization (RevIN) and channel-wise attention, optimized using sharpness-aware minimization (SAM).","experimental_setup":"Experiments were conducted on 8 publicly available datasets: ETTh1, ETTh2, ETTm1, ETTm2, Electricity, Exchange, Traffic, and Weather. The models were evaluated using MSE and MAE, with a look-back window of 512 and prediction horizons of 96, 192, 336, and 720.","limitations":"The paper does not explicitly discuss limitations but it can be inferred that the model's performance might be sensitive to the choice of the neighborhood size ρ in SAM, as indicated by the sensitivity analysis. The reliance on channel-wise attention may also limit its applicability to datasets where temporal dependencies are more critical.","future_research_directions":"The paper suggests future work could build upon the SAMformer architecture to improve it further, potentially by exploring different attention mechanisms or incorporating other techniques to stabilize training and improve generalization. Exploring the application of SAMformer to other time series forecasting tasks or datasets could also be a direction for future research."}
add_method_texts:
{"arxiv_id":"2108.03418v2","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2108.03418v2","title":"Information Bottleneck Approach to Spatial Attention Learning","authors":["Qiuxia Lai","Yu Li","Ailing Zeng","Minhao Liu","Hanqiu Sun","Qiang Xu"],"published_date":"2021-08-07T10:35:32Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"The selective visual attention mechanism in the human visual system (HVS)
restricts the amount of information to reach visual awareness for perceiving
natural scenes, allowing near real-time information processing with limited
computational capacity [Koch and Ullman, 1987]. This kind of selectivity acts
as an 'Information Bottleneck (IB)', which seeks a trade-off between
information compression and predictive accuracy. However, such information
constraints are rarely explored in the attention mechanism for deep neural
networks (DNNs). In this paper, we propose an IB-inspired spatial attention
module for DNN structures built for visual recognition. The module takes as
input an intermediate representation of the input image, and outputs a
variational 2D attention map that minimizes the mutual information (MI) between
the attention-modulated representation and the input, while maximizing the MI
between the attention-modulated representation and the task label. To further
restrict the information bypassed by the attention map, we quantize the
continuous attention scores to a set of learnable anchor values during
training. Extensive experiments show that the proposed IB-inspired spatial
attention mechanism can yield attention maps that neatly highlight the regions
of interest while suppressing backgrounds, and bootstrap standard DNN
structures for visual recognition tasks (e.g., image classification,
fine-grained recognition, cross-domain classification). The attention maps are
interpretable for the decision making of the DNNs as verified in the
experiments. Our code is available at https://github.com/ashleylqx/AIB.git.","github_url":"https://github.com/ashleylqx/AIB","main_contributions":"The paper introduces an Information Bottleneck (IB)-inspired spatial attention mechanism for visual recognition in DNNs. The key contributions include: (1) proposing a spatial attention module that minimizes the mutual information (MI) between the attention-modulated representation and the input while maximizing the MI between the attention-modulated representation and the task label, (2) designing a quantization module to round continuous attention scores to learnable anchor values, and (3) demonstrating improved interpretability of the attention mechanism for DNN decision-making compared to other spatial attention models.","methodology":"The methodology involves deriving an end-to-end trainable spatial attention mechanism from the Information Bottleneck principle. This includes a variational approximation of the IB principle within an attentive framework. The framework consists of an attention module p(a|x), a set of anchor values for quantization, an encoder p(z|x, a), and a decoder q(y|z). A quantization module is used to restrict the information bypassed by the attention map. The entire framework is trained end-to-end using a defined loss function.","experimental_setup":"The proposed method was evaluated on image classification (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100), fine-grained recognition (CUB-200-2011, SVHN), and cross-domain classification (STL-10). The models were built using VGG and Wide Residual Networks (WRN). The performance was compared against standard architectures and existing attention mechanisms. Interpretability was assessed through spatial and frequency domain modifications, measuring attention consistency between original and modified samples.","limitations":"The paper does not explicitly discuss limitations. However, the straight-through estimator used for gradient approximation during attention score quantization is a potential limitation, as it is a simple approximation. Additionally, the empirical setting of β, λq, and λc might limit the generalizability of the approach.","future_research_directions":"The paper does not explicitly suggest future research directions. However, potential extensions could involve: (1) exploring different quantization techniques beyond the nearest neighbor look-up, (2) investigating the application of this attention mechanism to other computer vision tasks like object detection and segmentation, and (3) developing more sophisticated gradient estimators for the quantization module to improve training stability and performance."}
